title: Israeli Airstrikes Kill 10 at Maghazi Refugee Camp After Israel-Backed Militia Raid
detail: At least 10 Palestinians were killed on April 7, 2026 in Israeli airstrikes on the Maghazi refugee camp in central Gaza after clashes erupted between an Israeli-backed militia and Hamas's Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades. Investigative outlet Drop Site News reported that the Israel-backed Abu Nasira militia attempted to abduct Hamas operatives in the camp, prompting residents and fighters to resist. Israeli drones and aircraft then carried out multiple airstrikes on three locations in Maghazi, including near a school housing displaced Palestinians, killing at least 10. The incident highlighted growing tensions around Israeli-backed armed factions operating inside Gaza under the terms of the fragile October 2025 ceasefire, and raised serious questions about accountability for civilian deaths during militia operations.

media.caption: Israeli airstrikes kill at least 10 near Gaza school at Maghazi refugee camp

title: WHO Suspends Gaza Medical Evacuations After Driver Killed by Israeli Fire
detail: WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus announced on April 7, 2026 the immediate suspension of all medical evacuations from Gaza after WHO contract driver Majdi Aslan, 54, was killed by Israeli forces in eastern Khan Younis the day before. Tedros confirmed Israeli troops fired on the WHO-rented vehicle and announced the halt to the medical evacuation program until further notice. The Israeli military stated its troops 'identified the vehicle as a threat.' France expressed 'deep concern' over the suspension, noting that patient evacuation is essential as Gazans lack adequate access to medical care within the Strip. Over 18,000 Palestinian patients are awaiting medical evacuations for treatment unavailable inside Gaza.

media.caption: WHO halts Gaza medical evacuations after Israeli forces kill WHO contractor

title: Protests Sweep West Bank Over Palestinian Authority Death Penalty Law
detail: Widespread protests erupted across the West Bank following the Palestinian Authority's enactment of a death penalty law, according to Al Jazeera's weekly wrap. Demonstrators condemned the legislation as authoritarian overreach, with rallies reported in multiple West Bank cities. Human rights groups have raised concerns that the law could be used to target political opponents and suppress dissent. The protests reflect deepening frustration with PA governance amid the ongoing conflict, adding internal political pressure to an already volatile situation in the occupied territories.

media.caption: Palestine weekly wrap: Protests sweep West Bank after death penalty law
